description: Links a subject to a predicate that identifies, describes, or locates it. 'nai' also asserts that an event or condition exists or occurs. It states the relation in the clause; it does not imply permanence, approval, or a deeper essence.
articulatory_notes: Set the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n' and let the air pass through the nose into stressed open central 'a'. Keep the next vowel separate: raise the tongue towards high-front 'i' without adding a glide between the two syllables.
sound_symbolism: The dental nasal gives this short copula a quiet voiced opening. Its two vowels remain plainly separate, so even the smallest Phi predicate has a little room inside it.
usage_notes: The predicate stands before 'nai': an adjective, noun, place phrase, or the deictic 'ha' or 'ra' can fill that position. Questions and tense keep the same frame. A clause-initial pronoun remains the subject, so 'mia lohau nai' means 'I am a dog', not 'my dog exists'.

semantic_domains.spatial: location stated through a place predicate
